title: 4th July Debate: Is 'Medical Aesthetics' Really Medical？ [Aesthetics Mastery Show]
filename: 4th July Debate： Is _Medical Aesthetics_ Really Medical？ [Aesthetics Mastery Show].en.vtt
excerpt: The World Health Organization actually defines health as a state of complete physical mental and social well-being and not nearly they're not merely the absence of disease so that's the first reason why health is absolutely involves making people feel better and but it's function…

segments.text: the World Health Organization actually defines health as a state of complete physical mental and social well-being and not nearly they're not merely the absence of disease so that's the first reason why health is absolutely involves making people feel better and but it's functioning better - welcome to the aesthetics master show I'm doctor Tim Pierce hi I'm Myranda piece and today off the back of these complex guidelines on when we can open and what kind of procedures we can do we're going to talk about what makes a procedure Medical versus cosmetic it cuts right to the heart of how our industry works and how its judged and seen by the external people who make decisions for us and so I thought worth diving into how you can be more medical in your practice as a finition and what it is that makes the difference between cosmetic and non cosmetic work so this is all come about because in

England it's different in the other UK countries undef initely elsewhere in the world post kovat we are no longer in lockdown and we've all been trying to decide can we go back to work because Boris our Prime Minister did not list us in the industries that can or can't go back but in that list was beauty therapists who couldn't go back but health care could so then it's this kind of like identity crisis almost you know are we health care what do you think well it'll be funny if you're watching this in America or in Australia I think it would be clear that I think it's clearer there that you wouldn't need me asking yourself the question am i abuse Alan because you wouldn't be you'd be a cynic and the reason it's different is because we have this lack of regulation but for other parts of the world that we really clear and but then the question that follows on from that is well what is the actual difference you know is there a difference does it

matter who's holding the needle in terms of how the what the procedure is and it's all the stuff surrounding that procedure and why the decision is made and how the guy is given that makes it into a medical procedure now the complication risks are also there because I know a lot of people were hearing while you're injecting someone's artery near someone's arteries and nerves and been absolutely there's a massive increase in risk between things we do particularly with injectables and other procedures that could be done in the same environment that our beauty treatments so risk is one element but actually there's a more important philosophical bit which is around is this procedure actually improving the health of the patient and that's where traditions should be making the decision decisions around that outcome and that's what's worth trying to figure out how do we make sure we're doing that what questions can you ask how can you differentiate yourself essentially from a purely cosmetic procedure in real practical terms how do you assure yourself that you are medical with the

questions you ask for example well the outcome of if you're making a decision from a clinical perspective so you're saying I'm a healthcare professional I'm making things that I'm justifying this treatment for a health outcome the health outcomes are around how you function in your life they aren't purely about do you like the look that you're achieving so um I think you'd be quite hard to make the argument that you know a tattoo on your neck is gonna make you a healthier individual for example now the person might really like it and feel happier with it but how do you justify the that they are actually functioning better not saying it's impossible I think is really hard how can we say if a treatment is for health reasons so the interesting thing that confuses people about this is that you can particularly where there's no regulation you could do the same procedure but one would be for health reasons one would not and that I think what people sometimes get confused by is they think that the risk itself is what makes it a procedure so if this gets if

I can cause a serious outcome from this a negative outcome or a complication that is what makes it a health procedure but it's actually not like that is why you need someone who is validated to do that procedure but it's not quite the same thing as a as doing it for medical or for health reasons if you're doing it for those reasons you to justify that the patient's life is going to be better because of the procedure there needs to be some sort of process where you're making the decision this the decision with the patient that something in their life will be so meaningfully different that they will function better essentially that's it so the World Health Organization actually defines health as a state of complete physical mental and social well-being and not nearly they're not merely the absence of disease so that's the first reason why health is absolutely involves making people feel better and but it's functioning better too so it's probably best if I give an actual story so I do have permission from the patient to share this story

which was someone who had damaged her nose from a cocaine addiction this damage represented shame to her so she couldn't function in her normal life so she would want to you know take the kids to school all the normal stuff that people do and it would prohibit her from doing that by fixing the little imperfection because it wasn't particularly big but by fixing it freed her to go and do those things so that would be a really good example of someone who had a an improvement to their their functioning of their life their health in other words their social and emotional well-being but it's much more practical than just feeling good about it so many people will have a procedure and say oh I just really like that I feel better but unless you've got some reasoning why they're functioning better and it could be argued that was purely cosmetic okay so go back I'm sorry I don't really understand the tattoo example is it because the tattooist isn't a medical professional and therefore they don't take well-being into account during the consultation

well tattooist has no obligation to see if this procedure will actually make the person function better in society like it I mean tattoos a good example because often you could argue that the tattoo makes people function worse than society if you tattoo a snake on someone's forehead it might affect their ability to get a job so that would be an example where it's actually not the tattooist responsibility whereas a medical professional it's clearly it is officially your responsibility to make sure that the work that you do makes people function better and we're regulated to make sure we're doing that there's one thing I think is lost in our not all debate in our industry because people go on about complications that's a really good reason why you want a healthcare professional to do it but really it's the system that is helping that is making sure that the treatments actually going to make you better so we're looking for specific things in the patient's life are better I can you know an example would be now I can take selfies without worrying about it I can i wedding photos and I don't have to worry about being seen from the side I

feel more confident I'm more self-possessed I'm communicating with people better because I'm no longer constantly having intrusive thoughts about them looking at me from a different angle there are a million different ways to do it and I think actually the most patients if you talk to them in depth they often that most of them will probably have a really good reason why this procedure is valid there are very few people who flippantly for no particular deep reason get stabbed in the face like it's painful it must be making a significant difference the job of a health of a clinician is to make sure it's making the difference that they want but they really want and that you agree it's in their best interests because there are also times where people are doing it for the wrong reasons and it doesn't actually make them happy it doesn't make them function better but the procedure might look great and that's where it gets really even more interesting but the key in this day and age if you're justifying a procedures particularly you're justifying opening you need to justify that you're doing procedures in the patient's best interests and even better if you can validate that somehow how could you do that so there are these questionnaires that you can do they're sort of patient happiness satisfaction

questionnaires and there are cosmetic procedures screening questionnaire that you can get I'm going to link some of those in the in some resources so we'll do a summary of this podcast with the take-home points and some links to some resources that might help you but essentially you're looking for anything that particularly the psychological assessment tools where you can show that the procedure is at the reason the patient is having the treatment is a significant problem for them and that you can solve that problem that's what we're looking for at least you may not it's not necessary that you always solve the problem because no medical treatment souls always solves every problem but that there's a good justification with a fair bet that it's going to improve the problem and is what you're looking for and most of them are going to be psychological because or I've even had debates with dermatologists who say things like we are not psychiatrists that might be true if you've got something like acne to treat because you can say the problem is there's a functional problem with the physiology of the skin but anything that has a cosmetic component you need to

have to a degree be interested in the psychology of the patient and to make sure that the that you're actually improving their health mentally and not making it worse or making no difference at all what about taking myself as an example so I have had lots of syringes of filler not since January but I think if I never come across this industry then maybe I would have different expectations about what was possible but now that I know that you know lovely subtle results that totally it hurts my well being are possible then when they run out it does play on my mind I'm like several times a day noticing in the mirror you know a particular line whatever and that plays in my mind and I do think it's my well I get the well-being by having that topped up but my question is like if I hadn't I don't know as you know I mean it's like if I hadn't had that would well that it needs to been something and I remember your first foray

into this there wasn't it was pure also about what you'd notice that was changing and getting you down it wasn't a purely like oh I might as well I work here yeah it was years before you even had a tree here you know seven or eight years and so something happened something should happen where you're and you feel it creating friction in your life and you're not and it can be as subtle as I don't feel self-possessed I'm self-conscious that makes me speak up less or it might make makes me just think things when I should be think about something else and some sort of difference in how you're functioning because of that of something that's changing and I think a lot of the stuff is you know it is it's often unconscious until you ask people about it so if one of the questions I often ask patients is when do you notice the problem and that's quite often in the morning when they're putting the makeup on and then how does that affect you and they'll often say well it you know I feel stressed in that process I don't enjoy it I used to enjoy it now it's a down and when I leave I'm constantly trying

to check my makeup again to try and cover the little lines or because there whatever the makeup gets in the creases and all these little stories hint at a friction that the problem is causing in their lives that if you can remove becomes a justification that they're actually functioning better now it is subtle some of it some of it's you know huge like the example of the nose and that was causing complete coming to a halt of that of normal life and those are the more obvious ones and obviously the more subtle it gets the more you might want to basically talk more and make sure you've got a good justification it's worth knowing that most patients won't actually know what it is that they're trying to get away from until you are tick until you ask them to articulate it and it's always there but it's never been said to themselves so they just think oh I'll just have my lips done and why like what's made you think it what was the process you went on and you will more often than not nine out of ten times maybe eight out of ten times the lips and there's something there's some

sort of friction point and for most of them so I don't like smiling because my top lip disappears is pretty common so I don't smile and then I've got no pictures of me smiling and or pictures to me with my kids yeah that's a that's a more concrete one what I love looking for is something really concrete that we can aim for because this is the ultimate reassurance that you're actually making a benefit to the health is you may I often make it part of the deal of the consultation if I do this procedure for you will you promise to get some pictures taken then with your kids we had a great one for the dots in life and I've got I've seen the pictures that made me really happy because you're like yeah closed-loop that life is different because of the procedure that we did and there's no argument about that and that's what you need to look for the stage beneath that are called level 4 of the consultation which is and it's not practical if not as clearly practical but they will tell you that they feel differently throughout the different parts of their life so I'm happier every day and I feel more confident I feel like people engaging with me more I feel

a bit more able to speak up but what they haven't got is the there's a specific moment which happened because this procedure allowed it so photographs with the family going out for dinner with someone you haven't been for years because you're worried about people seeing you in the restaurant all of those really concrete outcomes are great to look for and you become part of that story I mean I collect them now I've got loads of those stories of people doing things they would never have done had they not had the right procedure at the right time so that's what we're looking for so what do you think if you got any cases where you know you've made a real difference to patients lives if you can make them unidentifiable get permission and be lovely if you share them in the comments but otherwise what are we taking from this episode is there anything you disagree with let us know the comments don't forget to Like and subscribe and don't forget to download those resources which are linked in down below take care [Music] you [Music]
